did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses uncertainty-aware Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to perform binary melanoma detection classification on dermoscopic images, which is a dermatology/skin-cancer task. The study experiments with these AI models to analyze the behavior and performance of a proposed Trustworthiness Indicator.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a 'Trustworthiness Indicator' and evaluating it on specific datasets (ISIC for melanoma, EyePACS for diabetic retinopathy) using uncertainty-aware CNNs. It includes detailed methodology sections on image pre-processing, computing infrastructure, and statistical analysis (Pearson’s and Spearman’s correlations), along with specific results and limitations, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Towards trustworthy digital health: reliability of uncertainty-aware convolutional neural networks
brief_summary: This study proposes a Trustworthiness Indicator (TI) to numerically quantify the reliability and consistency of uncertainty-aware Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in digital health applications. The authors evaluated the TI using binary melanoma detection on dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset and diabetic retinopathy grading on ocular fundus images from the EyePACS dataset. The experimental approach involved comparing the TI's performance against standard classification metrics to explore correlations, weaknesses, and robustness across different clinical contexts. The results demonstrate that the TI effectively measures the consistency of internal model representations, complementing traditional explainability methods like SHAP or LIME. Ultimately, the study concludes that the TI provides actionable information on model robustness, fostering informed trust in AI-assisted clinical decision-making.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used in binary classification task on dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset. The study focuses on distinguishing melanoma (malignant) from benign lesions.
Used in binary classification task on dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset as the negative class for melanoma detection. Table 6 specifically reports trustworthiness pairs for the 'Benign class'.
Used in multiclass grading task on ocular fundus images from the EyePACS dataset. Classes include: 0 (No DR), 1 (Mild), 2 (Moderate), 3 (Severe), and 4 (Proliferative DR).

Mentioned in Figure 1 caption as features shown in retina images and in literature review (Jiang et al., 2020) as regions of interest for DR classification, but not as distinct output classes in the paper's own experiments.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification task on dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset to distinguish between malignant melanoma and benign skin lesions. Metrics reported include Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, and F1-score.

Multiclass classification task on ocular fundus images from the EyePACS dataset. The model predicts discrete grades: 0 (No DR), 1 (Mild), 2 (Moderate), 3 (Severe), and 4 (Proliferative DR). Metrics reported include overall Accuracy, macro/micro-averaged Precision, Recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A comprehensive collection of dermatologic images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive, containing dermoscopy images linked to confirmed clinical diagnoses between 2016 and 2020. The study used a manually refined subset of this dataset for binary melanoma detection.

use.training: Used for training uncertainty-aware CNNs on a manually refined subset for binary melanoma detection.
use.validation: Used for validation of the proposed Trustworthiness Indicator and model performance.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed Trustworthiness Indicator and model performance.
use.other: The study utilized a 'manually refined subset' of the public ISIC dataset.

datasets_used.name: EyePACS Dataset
datasets_used.description: A large set of high-resolution retinal images obtained from EyePACS, providing left and right field images for each subject. The images are labeled with an ID and eye side, and assessed for diabetic retinopathy presence on a scale from zero to four.

use.training: Used for training uncertainty-aware CNNs on a manually refined subset for multimodal diabetic retinopathy grading.
use.validation: Used for validation of the proposed Trustworthiness Indicator and model performance.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed Trustworthiness Indicator and model performance.
use.other: The study utilized a 'manually refined subset' of the public EyePACS dataset.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the statistical relationship between the Trustworthiness Indicator (TI) and traditional performance metrics (e.g., Accuracy, F1-score).
To assess the statistical relationship between the Trustworthiness Indicator (TI) and traditional performance metrics.
To evaluate the goodness of fit for linear regressions between TI and performance metrics, and to test if standard transformations (logit, arcsine, polynomial) could linearize the relationship.

statistical_methods_used.method: Standard transformations (logit, arcsine of proportions, second-order polynomial fits)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To attempt to linearize the relationship between the bounded Trustworthiness Indicator and classical metrics.

To compute the mean pairwise trustworthiness for each network and to analyze the dispersion (standard deviation) of TI values across different classes (malignant vs. benign) to assess model disagreement.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA) with p-values. It reported correlation coefficients (Pearson’s r, Spearman’s q) and coefficients of determination (R²) to assess relationships, noting that |r| < 0.25 and |q| < 0.20, but did not perform significance tests on these correlations.

conclusions: The proposed Trustworthiness Indicator (TI) effectively quantifies the reliability and consistency of internal model representations in uncertainty-aware CNNs, complementing traditional explainability methods like SHAP or LIME.

High predictive performance (e.g., accuracy, F1-score) does not necessarily correlate with high trustworthiness; for instance, models like XCeption and InceptionV3 achieved competitive F1-scores but showed lower TI values, indicating less agreement in internal reasoning.

The TI captures an orthogonal property of model behavior, specifically the robustness and generalizability of feature representations, as evidenced by models like AlexNet and DenseNet exhibiting high TI values despite not always leading in accuracy or recall.

The statistical relationship between the TI and classical performance metrics (accuracy, precision, F1-score) is weak or nonlinear, with local positive trends observed only for moderate TI values and breaking down for outlier architectures exhibiting overfitting or underfitting.

The TI provides actionable information on model robustness that can foster informed trust in AI-assisted clinical decision-making, potentially signaling caution or the need for a second opinion when TI scores are low.

The study demonstrates the applicability of the TI across different clinical contexts, specifically binary melanoma detection on dermoscopic images and multimodal diabetic retinopathy grading on ocular fundus images.
important_remarks: The study explicitly acknowledges that the EyePACS dataset contains noisy labels due to inter-observer variability in grading, which was not addressed by additional strategies.

The ISIC dataset exhibits class imbalance, with fewer melanoma cases compared to benign lesions; the authors did not apply balancing techniques as the focus was on evaluating the reliability indicator rather than maximizing predictive accuracy.
The analysis is restricted to CNN-based architectures, excluding more recent architectures such as Vision Transformers (ViTs) and hybrid radiomics-deep learning models.

The Trustworthiness Indicator (TI) was evaluated on only two specific clinical contexts (melanoma and diabetic retinopathy), limiting the generalizability of the findings to other digital health domains.

The study did not perform formal hypothesis testing (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA) with p-values; it relied on correlation coefficients (Pearson’s r, Spearman’s q) and coefficients of determination (R²) to assess relationships.

The proposed TI complements but does not replace traditional explainability methods like SHAP or LIME, as it quantifies internal representation consistency rather than feature attribution for individual predictions.

High predictive performance (e.g., accuracy, F1-score) does not necessarily correlate with high trustworthiness; models like XCeption and InceptionV3 achieved competitive F1-scores but showed lower TI values, indicating less agreement in internal reasoning.

The statistical relationship between the TI and classical performance metrics is weak or nonlinear, with local positive trends observed only for moderate TI values and breaking down for outlier architectures exhibiting overfitting or underfitting.
